me.text:
I’ve been feeling stressed with all the recent changes, including Monty’s role and my step back from leadership. This shift doesn’t fully align with my long-term career goals, as my intention has always been to be part of the leadership team. While I am comfortable with the commission structure moving forward, I do feel disappointed and saddened by no longer being considered a key member of leadership. It also feels difficult to reconcile this change with the results I delivered in December and the level of commitment and impact I had throughout 2025.

I’m not sure there is full awareness of how central my contribution has been to the business over the past year. Beyond Sales, I supported multiple critical areas: training Alketa on finance, stepping in to make payments and as an HR point of contact, handling high-stress situations with key personnel, and helping stabilize and move Sales forward. I also don’t think the complexity of leading Sales under last year’s conditions, particularly with Tulita, has been fully recognized.

My intention in sharing this is not to create conflict, but to bring clarity. Being part of the leadership team is important to me, and I want to make sure my role, impact, and long-term trajectory are aligned with that vision.

Staying on top of several major accounts. Customers are clearly feeling the value, which is very rewarding to hear.

highlights.text: I was on holidays last week, so I do not have direct personal deliverables to report for that period.

However, after reviewing the situation upon my return, it is clear that the team continued handling a high volume of Swype-related cases, especially around BlueBanc card cancellations, replacement links, provider changes, blocked/hobbled cards, and unclear refund or balance flows. These areas continue to have a direct impact on Support workload and CSAT.

progress.text: This week, my focus is on regaining full operational control after returning from holidays and reducing the increased ticket volume, especially for Swype cases.

The main priorities currently in flight are improving clarity around Swype support flows, including replacement cards, hobble mode, blocked cards, refunds, airtime balance issues, and provider-related escalations. I am also continuing to push for a stronger Help Center for both Swype and Reloadly, so the team and customers have better documentation and less dependency on individual knowledge.

A key operational concern is that when I am not available, the newer team members still struggle to handle the full ticket volume and prioritize correctly, especially when multitasking is required. This creates a dependency risk and affects our ability to maintain CSAT consistently.

On the Airtime/Rebtel side, we are also dealing with issues mainly linked to insufficient balance, Afghanistan traffic, and operator-side degradation. In many of these cases, the root cause is external, but we still need to maintain clear communication and visibility with customers and partners.

me.text:
I am back from holidays and doing okay, but I returned to a considerably higher ticket volume and several complex Swype-related operational issues.

The main challenge is the pressure created by unresolved or unclear processes with the new provider, combined with the dependency on me for the most complex cases. I expect to bring things more under control this week, but the situation is demanding and requires close follow-up.

highlights.text: Last week I was absent for several days, so my direct execution time was reduced. Even so, we completed the hiring process for Endrit and he is starting today. His onboarding process and calendar are already prepared, so we can make sure he ramps up properly and starts contributing as soon as possible.

On the support side, we closed Friday with the queue at almost 110 tickets, after reducing a significant amount of volume during the week. This was mainly driven by closing several Swype refund cases, moving KYC-related cases forward, and unblocking pending customer issues that had been open for some time.

progress.text: This week the focus is on stabilizing the support queue, especially after weekend/on-call shifts, where we are still seeing that the team does not fully understand some processes and the queue increases again. We need to reinforce ownership, documentation, and clarity around Swype, refunds, KYC, and escalation flows so that the queue does not build up again during lower-coverage periods.

I am also working on the support tooling review and whether we should migrate to a new support tool or continue improving the current setup. In parallel, I am finishing the Help Center article updates, and the goal is to have the Help Center updated this week.

me.text: I feel focused, but the main challenge is making sure the team has enough process clarity and autonomy so we do not keep losing progress during weekends or guard shifts.

I completed AI playbooks for outbound response handling, standardized our interview process and HubSpot hiring templates, finalized expandable conference banners, and resolved access across all company social channels so marketing execution is much more centralized moving forward.

progress.text: This week was focused on operational improvements that should compound over time. After reviewing inbound performance, we identified that more than 80% of leads filtered through the new qualification process are not sales-ready, which gives us a much clearer picture of where marketing and sales should spend their time.

I updated the sales pipeline to match our new onboarding approach, refined automated outbound messaging to improve conversion rates, continued building prospect lists to keep campaigns fed, and expanded internal documentation for sales note-taking and CRM management while training Nick Stephan on pipeline ownership. On the hiring side, I completed four marketing specialist interviews, advanced candidates to the next stage, and have additional interviews scheduled around SHRM next week while continuing to build a repeatable recruiting process.

me.text: Feeling energized and optimistic. highlights.text: SHRM was a strong proving ground for our event strategy. The experience provided a practical framework for scaling into larger industry events with greater confidence and efficiency.

progress.text: Several key initiatives are moving forward this week. highlights.text: Delivered a major reliability upgrade to order activation/redemption by implementing a resilient, step-based orchestration model with durable run/step/issue tracking, replay support, and better support visibility. This moves us from “best effort + logs” to deterministic recovery and operational traceability, which is foundational for a world-class partner experience.

progress.text: On the API side, activation is now consistently routed through the resilient engine (legacy path removed), with clearer activation contracts (activationStatus vs orderStatus), stronger validation/error consistency, and improved async processing via durable event publication + SQS listener flow. I also added internal BFF-facing support endpoints for activation run visibility/replay and branded progress events to support future real-time UX updates. In parallel, I tightened engineering discipline with documentation2.0 updates, CI guardrails (cognitive complexity/injection patterns), and standardized local developer MCP setup for faster debugging across DB/Redis/LocalStack.

me.text: Focused and energized. Workload is high but manageable, and I have good momentum because architecture, implementation, and documentation are aligned. Prioritizing execution discipline this week to convert the reliability foundation into measurable operational outcomes.

highlights.text: Delivered major new Swype platform milestones across the runtime API, BFFs, dashboards, website, docs, and infrastructure:

- Advanced B2C activation/runtime flow end to end: activation status/details, Auth0 activation profile enrichment, public error-message contracts, durable activation progress, language preference/i18n foundation, and legacy-aligned authenticated B2C dashboard experience.
- Built and hardened B2C card runtime: API routes, customer/profile alignment, card list parity, cursor pagination, secure sensitive card-detail reveal, and audit routing.

- Strengthened platform contract reliability: enforced cursor pagination guardrails, tightened public filter-scope contracts, fixed internal request logging continuity, and regenerated/synced API docs.

- Moved B2B partner workflows forward: dashboard card route boundary, freeze attribution visibility, transaction cursor pagination UI, KYB submitted/pending approval flow, and Persona approval gating.

- Shipped supporting infra/governance work: B2C BFF/internal client infra, trusted client rollout records, required runtime config placeholders, static hosting/deploy hardening, and branch cleanup/token support.

progress.text: Entering this week, the new platform is converging across runtime, BFF, and frontend surfaces. Current focus:
- Close remaining B2C dashboard parity gaps and validate activation, cards, and account flows against Swype API and B2C BFF contracts.
- Finish live-mode/KYB approval gating alignment across B2B BFF and dashboard so partners see the correct pending/approved state.
- Continue contract hardening: cursor pagination everywhere, stable public error messages, docs/OpenAPI hash guardrails, and SDK-impact review where API behavior changes.
- Continue infra rollout validation for BFF/internal clients, Cognito/Auth0 routing, API Gateway/WAF/static hosting, and deployment gates.
- Keep reducing operational risk with tests, docs, receipts, and cross-repo ownership-boundary checks.
